items.correct: Rapid alternation between the written note and the note a step above.
items.why: Students confuse trill (rapid alternation between two notes) with vibrato (pitch oscillation around one note). Trills add ornamentation; vibrato adds expressive width to a sustained pitch.

items.correct: Two or more independent melodic lines sounding together.
items.misconception: A single melody with chord accompaniment.
items.why: Students confuse polyphony with thick texture or homophony. Polyphony specifically means INDEPENDENT MELODIES - like a fugue. Melody + chords is HOMOPHONY, not polyphony.

items.q: What distinguishes Classical (1750-1820) from Baroque style?
items.correct: Clearer melodic phrases, homophonic texture, balanced structures like sonata form.
items.random_wrong: Classical means any old serious music.
items.why: Students use 'classical' loosely. The Classical period specifically reacted against Baroque complexity - simpler textures, clearer cadences, and balanced phrase structures (Mozart, Haydn, early Beethoven).

items.q: Whose 1967 Treatise is a famous graphic score?
items.why: It's Cornelius Cardew. “John Cage” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was MIDI standardised?
items.why: Correct answer: 1983. “1979” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Around what year did Les Paul pioneer multitrack recording?
items.why: The real figure is 1947 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
